attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for her first antenatal visit at eight weeks gestation. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy multivitamins. She reports feeling anxious about the pregnancy but denies any current mental health concerns. She works as an accountant and lives with her partner. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits.

As part of routine antenatal screening, she completes the Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale and scores 14 out of 30. Her score to question 10 is negative.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Recommend online cognitive behavioural therapy only
Commence selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor therapy
Reassure and review at next routine antenatal visit
Refer for psychological assessment and support
attributes.explanation:
The Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) is a validated screening tool for both antenatal and postnatal depression. A score of 13 or more suggests an increased likelihood of depression and warrants further assessment. The tool helps identify women who may need additional support during pregnancy and the postpartum period.

Referral for psychological assessment and support is the most appropriate next step for this woman. Her EPDS score of 14 indicates she is at increased risk of depression, but she is not expressing immediate safety concerns that would necessitate urgent intervention. A mental health professional can conduct a comprehensive assessment and determine the most appropriate treatment plan.

Reassurance and review at the next routine visit would be inadequate given her elevated EPDS score. Delaying intervention could lead to worsening symptoms and potentially affect both maternal and fetal outcomes.

Commencing selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor therapy may eventually be appropriate, but should only be initiated after a thorough mental health assessment and discussion of treatment options. Starting medication should not be the first-line response to an elevated screening score.

Arranging urgent psychiatric assessment would be appropriate if there were immediate safety concerns or severe symptoms, which are not evident in this case. The presentation appears to be suitable for non-urgent mental health review.

Online cognitive behavioural therapy alone would be insufficient given her EPDS score. While digital mental health resources can be helpful, they should be part of a comprehensive care plan that includes professional support.

Regular mental health screening is an essential component of antenatal care. The EPDS should be administered at booking and again around 28 weeks gestation, or more frequently if concerns arise. Early identification and management of mental health issues during pregnancy can significantly improve outcomes for both mother and baby.
learningPoints: The Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale is validated for use both during pregnancy and postpartum.
A score of 13 or more on the Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale indicates increased risk of depression requiring further assessment.
Regular mental health screening should occur throughout pregnancy, particularly at booking and 28 weeks gestation.
A positive score on question 10 identifies a risk for suicide/self harm and requires further assessment regardless of overall score.
references: Queensland Health. Perinatal mental health: Queensland clinical guidelines [Internet]. Brisbane: Queensland Government; 2023 [cited 2024 Nov 22]. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0027/1321929/f-peri-mental-health.pdf

King Edward Memorial Hospital (KEMH). Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) and perinatal mental health referral [Internet]. Perth: Government of Western Australia; 2018 [cited 2024 Nov 22]. Available from: https://www.kemh.health.wa.gov.au/~/media/HSPs/NMHS/Hospitals/WNHS/Documents/Clinical-guidelines/CMP/EPDS-and-Perinatal-Mental-Health-Referral.pdf
